q: Photometry is the science of measuring visible light in units that are weighted according to the sensitivity of the human eye.
The human eye responds only to light having wavelengths between about $400 nm$ and $700 nm$.
The human eye is more sensitive to green light of $555 nm$ compared to other colors.
Luminous flux ($\\Phi$) is a measurement of the power of visible light produced by a light source.
One lumen ($1 lm$) is equivalent to $683$ watts of light with a wavelength of $555 nm$.
Luminous intensity ($I_v$) is a measure of the luminous flux emitted by a light source per unit solid angle.
The candela ($cd$) is the SI basic unit for luminous intensity.
In the SI system, a solid angle is expressed in steradian ($sr$).
The solid angle subtended at the centre by the whole surface area of a sphere is $2\\pi$ steradian.
Luminance ($L_v$) is the luminous intensity per unit area of light travelling in a given direction.
The SI unit for luminance is candela per metre squared ($cd \\cdot m^{-2}$).
Illuminance ($E_v$) is the total luminous flux incident on a surface per unit area.
The SI unit for illuminance is lux ($lx$).
One lux is equal to one lumen per metre squared ($1 lm \\cdot m^{-2}$).
The minimum average illuminance for reading areas in school study halls should be $500 lux$.
The inverse square law of illuminance states that illuminance is directly proportional to the distance $r$.
